content:
First, note that comparing degrees shows that any non‐constant solution must be linear.  Write
\[f(x)=ax+b,\quad a\neq0.\]
Then
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
= a(x-1)+b + (ax+b)+(a(x+1)+b)
=3ax+3b,
\]
while
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{(ax+b)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{a^2x^2+2abx+b^2}{2013x}
=\frac{a^2x+2ab+\tfrac{b^2}{x}}{2013}.
\]
For this to be a polynomial identity (valid for all nonzero \(x\)), the \(1/x\)–term on the right must vanish, so \(b^2=0\) and hence \(b=0\).  Thus \(f(x)=ax\), and the equation becomes
\[
3ax=\frac{a^2x}{2013}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3a=\frac{a^2}{2013}.
\]
Since \(a\neq0\), we get \(a=3\cdot2013=6039\).  Hence the unique nonconstant solution is
\[
f(x)=6039x,
\]
and so \(f(1)=6039\).  Therefore the sum of all possible values of \(f(1)\) is
\[
\boxed{6039}.
\]

=3ax+3b,

\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{(ax+b)^2}{2013x}
=\frac{a^2x^2+2abx+b^2}{2013x}
=\frac{a^2x+2ab+\tfrac{b^2}{x}}{2013}.
\]
For this to be a polynomial identity (valid for all nonzero \(x\)), the \(1/x\)–term on the right must vanish, so \(b^2=0\) and hence \(b=0\).  Thus \(f(x)=ax\), and the equation becomes

\[
\boxed{6039}.
